title: Who are the ‘fire-taming’ healers of modern France? | Aeon Essays
gist: An unregulated, word-of-mouth network of thousands of French folk 'fire-tamers' is informally embedded in mainstream oncology and burns wards, riding a global institutional shift (WHO) toward integrating traditional medicine while raising the unresolved tension between placebo/relational care and dangerous health misinformation.

insights.claim: More than 5,000 traditional healers operate in France and at least two-thirds of the French population have consulted such a practitioner, indicating folk medicine is a mainstream rather than fringe behavior.
insights.speaker: Susanna Crossman
insights.so_what: Mass adoption of unregulated traditional care signals a large, durable demand pool that conventional health systems and wellness brands are not capturing.
insights.quote: there are more than 5,000 traditional healers in France today, and at least two-thirds of the French population have consulted such a practitioner

insights.claim: Fire-taming is entirely unregulated in France, with no official statistics, organisation, or diploma, leaving the practice invisible to statistical and oversight systems.
insights.speaker: Susanna Crossman
insights.so_what: A care market with no paper trail evades both regulation and measurement, a blind spot for health policy and for anyone modeling actual care delivery.
insights.quote: there are no official statistics about fire-taming, no organisation or diploma to validate or regulate this treatment

insights.claim: French hospitals informally embed fire-tamers in patient care protocols, distributing contact lists for radiotherapy and burns patients despite the practice having no official status.
insights.speaker: Susanna Crossman
insights.so_what: Shows that even highly codified, evidence-based systems run shadow practices when patient demand and perceived relational benefit are strong.
insights.quote: In French hospitals, fire-tamers have an unofficial yet common role in the patient care protocol.

insights.claim: In a 2007 survey of nine clinics in Haute-Savoie, seven had an unofficial fire-taming protocol, with the Annecy emergency department's own staff performing fire-taming inside the hospital.
insights.speaker: Susanna Crossman
insights.so_what: Quantifies how pervasive the informal practice is even within institutional medicine, not just at its margins.
insights.quote: Seven of the nine clinics and hospitals he contacted had an unofficial fire-taming protocol
insights.locator: In a 2007 study, the French doctor Nicolas Perret

insights.claim: A 2017 Auvergne study of 500 radiotherapy patients found more than half consulted a fire-tamer during treatment, showing concurrent use of folk and biomedical care is the norm, not the exception.
insights.speaker: Susanna Crossman
insights.so_what: Patients routinely blend modalities; demand for adjunct/complementary care persists alongside, not instead of, frontline treatment.
insights.quote: A French study of 500 radiotherapy patients in Auvergne from 2017 showed that more than half of them consulted a fire-tamer during treatment.

insights.claim: Perret's research found that when patients believe in the 'secret', 87 per cent experienced a reduction in pain, making belief the central operative variable of the practice.
insights.speaker: Susanna Crossman
insights.so_what: Belief-mediated pain reduction is a measurable effect; expectation and trust are quantifiable inputs to outcomes, relevant to any care or product whose value is partly experiential.
insights.quote: when patients believe in the ‘secret’, 87 per cent of them experience a reduction in pain

insights.claim: The evidence-based explanation for fire-taming is placebo arising from the caregiver-patient relationship, with emotional state shown to improve immune response.
insights.speaker: Susanna Crossman
insights.so_what: Frames the relational/attention component of care as the active ingredient, a transferable insight for service design where trust drives outcomes.
insights.quote: The explanation that evidence-based medicine gives for fire-taming is that it is a placebo, connected to the relationship between caregiver and patient.

insights.claim: Psychiatrist Amir Levine links feeling safe to a lowered stress response that reduces inflammation, providing a physiological bridge between relational care and the inflammatory conditions fire-tamers target.
insights.speaker: Susanna Crossman
insights.so_what: Suggests a real biological pathway (safety lowers stress lowers inflammation) underlying perceived effects of relational/folk care.
insights.quote: When we feel safe, the whole stress response goes down, which goes with inflammation and all that stuff.
insights.locator: Karine the nurse manager is steeped

insights.claim: The WHO states traditional, complementary and integrative medicine is practised in 170 countries and used by billions, marking an official institutional reversal of the secularization thesis that ancient knowledge would become redundant.
insights.speaker: Susanna Crossman
insights.so_what: A global, institutionally-sanctioned move toward integrative medicine signals a structurally growing market and legitimacy for traditional/complementary care.
insights.quote: Traditional, complementary and integrative medicine is practised in 170 countries and is used by billions of people.

insights.claim: In 2025 the WHO opened a digital platform consolidating 1.6 million resources on traditional medicine, from scientific studies to Indigenous knowledge.
insights.speaker: Susanna Crossman
insights.so_what: Institutional infrastructure-building (a 1.6M-resource platform) is concrete evidence that integrative medicine is being formalized at scale.
insights.quote: the WHO opened a digital platform consolidating 1.6 million resources on the topic

insights.claim: Academics observed a rise in health misinformation since COVID-19 as people turn away from conventional medicine, the central downside risk of legitimizing alternative practices.
insights.speaker: Susanna Crossman
insights.so_what: The same trust dynamics that make folk care attractive also fuel dangerous misinformation, the key tail risk for the wellness and integrative-medicine sectors.
insights.quote: academics and medical professionals have observed a rise in health misinformation as people turn away from conventional medicine
insights.locator: The wariness of hospitals is understandable

insights.claim: The healers themselves disclaim curing life-threatening illness and self-limit their scope, distinguishing benign relational folk practice from predatory false-cure quackery.
insights.speaker: Susanna Crossman
insights.so_what: The benign/harmful boundary turns on whether a practitioner discourages conventional treatment, the practical line for regulators and clinicians.
insights.quote: None of the healers I meet claim that they can cure life-threatening illness
insights.locator: By day, Stéphane works for an inland

insights.claim: Fire-tamers operate outside the cash economy, working largely for free or accepting tokens (10-20 euros, eggs, apples) because charging money is believed to destroy the 'gift', creating a non-monetary care network.
insights.speaker: Susanna Crossman
insights.so_what: A motivated, free-of-charge informal labor force fills care gaps that priced systems leave open, a structural feature of folk-care supply.
insights.quote: None appear interested in monetary gains. Some heal for free, or take whatever people give them: €10 or €20, eggs, a crate of freshly picked apples.
insights.locator: All of the fire-tamers I meet express

insights.claim: In Switzerland fire-tamers ('secret-makers') are on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list, showing formal cultural legitimation of the practice in a wealthy Western state.
insights.speaker: Susanna Crossman
insights.so_what: State-level cultural recognition lends durability and protection to practices that rationalist modernization predicted would vanish.
insights.quote: their practice is on UNESCO’s Intangible Cultural Heritage list
